name: Steel
maintenance_status: Active. The public steel-dev/steel-browser repository was created in November 2024, is Apache-2.0 licensed, had about 7.3k stars, was pushed on 2026-07-06, and its latest listed release was v0.5.3-beta on 2026-04-24. Recent commits in June and July 2026 include network instrumentation, CDP/fingerprint fallback fixes, request-header cleanup, and log durability fixes.
license: Apache-2.0 for the open-source steel-browser repository. This permissive license is compatible with ApplyPilot's AGPL-3.0 runtime as an external dependency or self-hosted service, but Steel Cloud usage is also governed by Steel's hosted service terms and privacy policy.
pricing_model: [uncertain] Steel's official pricing pages are not fully synchronized. The docs pricing table last edited 2025-05-30 lists Hobby, Starter, Developer, Pro, and Enterprise with browser-hour rates of $0.10, $0.10, $0.08, $0.05, and custom; paid captcha rates of $4, $3.50, and $3 per 1,000; and paid proxy bandwidth rates of $10, $8, and $5 per GB. The public homepage instead advertises Launch at $0/month plus usage with $30 one-time credits, Scale at $250/month plus usage with $100 monthly credits, and Enterprise custom. Treat the minute-metered browser-hour rates as planning inputs until the account dashboard confirms the current contract.
marginal_cost_per_attempt: [uncertain] For Steel Cloud alone, a 3 to 8 minute ApplyPilot browser session would add about $0.005 to $0.013 at $0.10/browser-hour, plus proxy bandwidth and captcha charges if enabled. At $0.05/browser-hour this falls to about $0.0025 to $0.0067. This excludes the dominant ApplyPilot LLM/agent cost; using the same agent on Steel would usually add a small browser-infrastructure charge rather than remove model spend.
projected_cost_per_verified_apply: [uncertain] A fresh 2026-07-09 read-only ApplyPilot cost report shows 909 applied rows across 6,371 terminal attempts (14.27%) and $2.0405 all-in recorded cost per successful apply. Using Steel's stale/conflicting public rate inputs, a 3-8 minute session costs about $0.0025-$0.0133; if every terminal attempt incurred that cost and completion stayed unchanged, the mechanical sensitivity is about $2.06-$2.13 per successful apply before proxy, model, subscription-amortization, and human-recovery changes. This is not a forecast because Steel-specific completion and fallback deltas are unmeasured.
billing_granularity: [uncertain] Steel docs state browser hours are billed by the minute and rounded up. Sessions default to a 5 minute timeout, can be created with a timeout parameter, can use inactivityTimeout to release idle sessions early, and should be explicitly released. Plan limits in the docs include daily requests, requests per second, concurrent sessions, data retention, and max session time; exact current plan names and credits should be verified in the dashboard because official public pages conflict.
model_cost: Steel itself is browser infrastructure, not the answering model. Model cost remains whatever ApplyPilot uses for the actor, answerer, verifier, or fallback agent. If Steel is used only as a remote CDP browser for current Playwright/MCP or Selenium flows, expected model spend is unchanged; only lower failure/retry rates or deterministic adapter routing reduce model cost.
infrastructure_cost: [uncertain] Managed Steel Cloud externalizes Chrome hosting, session storage, live viewer, traces, profiles, optional proxies, captcha support, and region capacity into metered browser-hour and usage pricing. Self-hosting moves cash cost to ApplyPilot's machine or cloud VM: Steel's Docker docs require Docker 20.10+, at least 4 GB RAM, and 10 GB disk, and Steel Local is documented as concurrency 1 versus Steel Cloud at 100+.
human_recovery_cost: [uncertain] Steel can reduce operator friction through interactive debug URLs, embedded live sessions, human takeover, reusable contexts/profiles, and beta credentials/TOTP support. It does not eliminate ApplyPilot's need for owner-supervised decisions on login gates, OTP ambiguity, sensitive questions, or final-submit review. Expected recovery minutes are unmeasured on ApplyPilot flows.
adapter_maintenance_tco: [uncertain] Medium. Steel can simplify remote browser provisioning and provide traces, but ApplyPilot still owns ATS adapters, answer provenance, required-field validation, deduplication, and submit confirmation. Additional maintenance includes Steel API keys, session/profile lifecycle, result-event session IDs, cost controls, cloud data-boundary review, and self-host/cloud failover handling.
verified_completion_rate: [uncertain] No primary Steel source publishes a verified job-application completion rate under Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday, and long-tail ATS conditions. The fresh ApplyPilot control baseline is 14.27% across all terminal fleet attempts (909/6,371), but that mixed-route rate is not a Steel estimate. Treat Steel as unmeasured until a matched canary compares local Chrome versus Steel with the same route, model, and queue sample.
failure_modes: Expected failure modes include provider session creation failure, timeout or idle leakage, cloud/browser crash, CDP or WebDriver connection loss, profile upload or persistence failure, auth gate, OTP wait, captcha or challenge, proxy/network reputation mismatch, unsupported required fields, agent no-result, ambiguous submit state, missing confirmation evidence, and site policy restrictions. Steel does not by itself solve ApplyPilot's answer-quality or exactly-once problems.
crash_recovery: Steel sessions have Live, Released, and Failed states; failed sessions are automatically cleaned up. Explicit release and releaseAll are available, and Agent Traces expose a timeline, video sync, details, and markdown/JSON/ZIP export for debugging. This is good for post-mortem and replay-as-code workflows, but deterministic resume after a mid-form crash still has to be implemented by ApplyPilot.
exactly_once_submit_safety: Weak by default. Steel provides browser execution and evidence capture, not application-level duplicate detection. ApplyPilot must keep its existing dedup_key/applied_set guards, pre-submit checkpoints, final-action boundary, positive confirmation requirement, and crash_unconfirmed quarantine. Steel traces can help adjudicate ambiguous outcomes but should not be the source of truth for marking applied.
verification_evidence_tier: Potential evidence tiers are confirmation DOM and URL, screenshots, video, console/network logs, Agent Trace timeline, and exported trace JSON; delayed email confirmation remains outside Steel and must be joined through ApplyPilot's email/outcome reconciliation. ATS application IDs are available only if the ATS exposes them in page, network, or email evidence.
verification_latency: [uncertain] Synchronous browser evidence should be available during or immediately after the session, and Agent Traces are useful for post-run review. Email-based confirmation and OTP workflows remain delayed and external. Steel-specific trace availability and retention vary by plan, so benchmark latency should record session end, trace availability, and email reconciliation times separately.
headless_headed_parity: [uncertain] Steel runs cloud Chrome sessions that can be driven through Playwright over CDP, Puppeteer over CDP, or Selenium over WebDriver, with live viewer/human takeover support. It is not the same environment as ApplyPilot's local headed Chrome profile, and public docs do not provide ATS-specific parity results. Compare headed local Chrome, Steel CDP, and Steel Selenium on the same synthetic and live canary forms.
python_integration: Good. The docs show a Python SDK using `from steel import Steel`, Playwright Python via `chromium.connect_over_cdp`, and Selenium 4 Python via a Steel WebDriver endpoint with per-request API-key and session headers. This is compatible with ApplyPilot's Python runtime, though async/sync Playwright choices and lifecycle cleanup must be wired carefully.
playwright_cdp_reuse: Partial. Existing Playwright logic can connect to a Steel session over CDP, but the endpoint is Steel's cloud WSS URL rather than ApplyPilot's local Chrome CDP port. Local Chrome profiles are not reused in place; Steel provides session context capture and Profiles API for portable state, with separate storage, size, and retention constraints.
migration_effort: Medium. A narrow canary can be added as a remote browser provider behind the existing Playwright/launcher path, recording Steel session ID, debug URL, trace URL, browser provider, and per-session cost. Broad adoption requires profile migration, data-boundary review, timeout/release discipline, hosted-service cost caps, and comparison against local Chrome failure modes.
ats_fit: [uncertain] Steel is best evaluated as infrastructure for browser-heavy routes and long-tail debugging, not as an ATS-specific submitter. It should work technically with Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday, and long-tail hosts through Chrome automation, but Workday tenant auth and account gating remain host-policy problems. Greenhouse/Ashby deterministic adapters likely benefit more from direct local or remote Playwright than from Steel specifically.
deterministic_coverage: [uncertain] Steel does not increase deterministic coverage on its own. Deterministic completion share remains driven by ApplyPilot's ATS adapters and answer-planning logic. Steel could host those adapters remotely, but direct ATS HTTP, browser-assisted HTTP, Greenhouse, and future Ashby adapters are the coverage levers.
escape_rate: [uncertain] Expected escape rate is similar to the current route for unsupported or semantic fields unless Steel materially lowers browser/runtime failures. Forms with unknown required questions, auth gates, captchas, login walls, unsupported file upload behavior, or ambiguous confirmations still need fallback to premium agent, supervised owner profile, or human review.
control_plane_overhead: Steel adds session create/release API calls, remote CDP or WebDriver round trips, and cloud trace/profile serialization. It removes local Chrome launch/display management and can make debugging easier through live viewer and traces. Selenium over Steel adds HTTP round trips per WebDriver command; Playwright over CDP should be preferred for existing ApplyPilot-style action density.
observability_joinability: Strong if instrumented. Steel session IDs, debug URLs, traces, video, network/console logs, profile IDs, and API costs can be joined to ApplyPilot's apply_queue, apply_result_events, llm_usage, worker_id, route, and email evidence. ApplyPilot must persist those IDs explicitly; otherwise Steel's dashboard evidence will not be queryable with queue outcomes.
persistent_profiles: Steel Profiles API can persist auth, cookies, extensions, credentials, and browser settings across sessions by saving the browser user data directory. The docs note a 300 MB profile size limit, FAILED state if upload fails, and automatic deletion after 30 days of non-use. Persisting updates is not automatic unless sessions are started with the right persistProfile/profileId behavior.
existing_session_attach: Steel supports attaching automation and viewers to Steel-created sessions through WebSocket/CDP, WebDriver, sessionViewerUrl, and debugUrl. It is not a safe direct attach mechanism for the user's already-authenticated local Chrome tab or profile; ApplyPilot would need to create or import managed Steel profile state instead.
login_and_otp: [uncertain] Steel supports reusable auth context, Profiles API, interactive human takeover, beta Credentials API with TOTP support, and optional captcha detection/solving. ApplyPilot should still route sensitive login, OTP relay, and owner decisions through its existing home/owner auth lanes and should not rely on Steel to bypass access controls or site restrictions.
extension_support: Supported but beta in Steel Cloud through the Extensions API. Extensions can be uploaded as .zip/.crx or from Chrome Web Store and attached to sessions; Steel Local can load extensions from the local extension folder. This is promising for narrowly permissioned ApplyPilot instrumentation, but beta status requires canary isolation.
session_data_boundary: [uncertain] In Steel Cloud, cookies, profiles, credentials metadata, screenshots, video, traces, network logs, files, and browser state can be transmitted to or retained by Steel according to plan retention and service terms. Self-hosting keeps more data under ApplyPilot control but loses or limits some cloud features such as credentials and high concurrency. This needs explicit approval before using real candidate accounts or resumes.
required_field_completeness: Steel does not provide required-field completeness by itself. ApplyPilot adapters, DOM inspectors, answer planners, and verifiers must enumerate required fields, detect unmapped controls, and refuse incomplete plans before final submit. Steel's traces help audit failures after the fact.
answer_provenance: Steel has no built-in guarantee that answers come from the user's resume, profile, or approved-answer corpus. ApplyPilot must keep answer provenance constraints in the answerer/verifier layer and should log answer source IDs alongside any Steel session evidence.
unsupported_question_fallback: Unsupported, sensitive, or free-text questions should fall back to ApplyPilot's verified answerer, premium agent, or human review. Steel can provide an interactive browser for human correction, but the policy for refusing or escalating unsupported answers remains entirely ApplyPilot-owned.
prompt_injection_resistance: Steel is a browser control layer, so prompt-injection resistance depends on the agent and tool policy wrapped around it. ApplyPilot should keep domain allowlists, no-secret-in-DOM rules, restricted extensions, minimal tool permissions, hostile-page tests, and a hard boundary around credentials and final submit actions.
site_permission_and_terms: [uncertain] Steel publishes legal links to its terms and privacy policy, but site-by-site permission remains ApplyPilot's responsibility. Use Steel only for legitimate browser automation where the job site, ATS, account, and user consent allow it. Do not treat proxy, stealth, captcha, or cloud-browser features as permission to bypass access controls or restrictions.
irreversible_action_guard: Steel does not supply an application-domain submit guard. ApplyPilot must enforce pre-submit checkpoints, complete answer plan validation, deduplication, route policy, canary caps, and positive confirmation capture. Human takeover should be available for uncertain flows, and crash_unconfirmed rows should stay quarantined.
concurrency: [uncertain] Docs pricing limits list concurrent sessions of 5 for Hobby, 10 for Starter, 20 for Developer, 100 for Pro, and custom for Enterprise; Steel Local vs Cloud docs summarize Local as 1 and Cloud as 100+. The homepage advertises Enterprise at 1,000+ concurrent sessions. Confirm account-specific concurrency before capacity planning.
startup_latency: [uncertain] Public docs do not provide cold or warm session startup latency. Measure session create time, CDP attach time, first page interactive time, profile load time, and trace availability in the ApplyPilot canary. Warm profile sessions may reduce login friction but can add upload/download persistence time.
warm_session_reuse: Supported through session context capture and Profiles API. Context transfer can preserve cookies/local storage across sessions, while Profiles persist the broader browser user data directory. Sessions should still be short-lived and explicitly released; idle waiting can be controlled with inactivityTimeout.
tracing_and_replay: Good for observability. Agent Traces provide a timeline of activities with page URL and timestamp, video sync, details, and export as markdown, JSON, or ZIP with screenshots. This supports debugging, audit, and reproduction-as-code, but it is not a deterministic replay engine and needs ApplyPilot-side evidence joins.
deployment_modes: Managed Steel Cloud, Docker self-hosting, one-click/hosted templates such as Railway or Render, local Docker Compose, and enterprise/custom cloud arrangements. Self-hosted mode can expose API and CDP ports locally; Steel Cloud provides higher concurrency, multi-region, managed proxies, credentials, files, and advanced cloud features.
browser_version_matrix: Primarily Chrome/Chromium. Steel supports Playwright and Puppeteer through CDP and Selenium through a W3C WebDriver-compatible endpoint for Steel sessions. It is not a Firefox or WebKit route for ApplyPilot and should be compared as a Chromium provider.
canary_stop_loss: Steel provides timeout, inactivityTimeout, session release, plan limits, and concurrency controls. ApplyPilot still needs per-route budget caps, max session minutes, max failed attempts, host-policy parking, canary remaining counts, no-submit-on-uncertain-result, and automatic halt on trace/session creation failures.
benchmark_design: Run a matched ATS-stratified benchmark against the current local Chrome route: Greenhouse, Ashby, Lever, Workable, Workday tenants, and long-tail hosts. Hold model, prompt, queue sample, profile state, and submit policy constant; compare local Playwright/CDP versus Steel Playwright/CDP and, optionally, Steel Selenium. Include synthetic forms for required fields, uploads, confirmation pages, login gates, and crash recovery.
route_funnel: Eligible job -> host policy -> local deterministic adapter or Steel canary route -> profile/context selection -> session create and attach -> pre-submit plan completeness check -> final submit or refusal -> confirmation evidence capture -> email reconciliation when needed -> final disposition. Any auth gate, unsupported required field, missing confirmation, or session crash should park, fallback, or quarantine rather than blind retry.
step_context_budget: [uncertain] Steel does not define an LLM context budget. For ApplyPilot, track CDP/WebDriver actions, DOM/accessibility tokens passed to the agent, screenshots, trace export size, network events, model tokens, and tool calls. Steel traces may reduce human debug time but do not reduce model context unless ApplyPilot changes the agent loop.
cache_replay_yield: [uncertain] Profiles and context reuse may reduce repeated login and setup steps, but no ApplyPilot measurement exists. Separately, Agent Trace exports can help generate deterministic scripts for repeated flows, yet no-model replay yield will come from ApplyPilot adapters, not Steel alone. Measure warm profile hit rate, profile invalidation, login reuse, and adapter replay success.
confidence_intervals: [uncertain] No confidence interval is available from public sources. A useful first canary would run at least 30 to 50 attempts per high-volume ATS bucket per route where legally and operationally allowed, report Wilson intervals for verified completion, bootstrap cost per verified apply, and separately track ambiguous outcomes.
historical_bucket_impact: [uncertain] The fresh ApplyPilot report shows 1,894 agent/browser-runtime failures costing $310.2143 and 2,042 preflight/policy failures costing $289.3490; fleet cost per apply is $14.6340 for Workday, $3.1846 for other/long-tail hosts, $1.6495 for Greenhouse, and $0.7751 for Ashby. Steel can plausibly affect paid no_result_line, browser launch, CDP loss, and crash_unconfirmed failures, but not expired or policy-ineligible work. Credit savings only when a canary lowers runtime failures without weakening positive confirmation quality.
recommendation: Canary, not broad adoption. Use Steel as a hosted-browser comparator and possible failover for remote browser stability, observability, and human takeover; do not expect it to beat deterministic ATS adapters on cost. The first production candidate is a small Steel Playwright/CDP canary on a stable ATS slice with strict cost caps, trace joins, and no relaxation of ApplyPilot's exactly-once submit safeguards.
